events.title: Caracas Junta Proclaimed
events.detail: On April 19, 1810, the Caracas Cabildo deposed Captain-General Vicente Emparan and established a Junta Suprema Conservadora de los Derechos de Fernando VII, initiating Venezuela's independence movement.

events.title: Venezuela Declares Independence
events.detail: On July 5, 1811, the Venezuelan Congress signed the Declaration of Independence — the first in Spanish South America. Francisco de Miranda led military forces; Simón Bolívar was a key conspirator. Spain refused to recognize the new republic.

events.title: Battle of Carabobo — Final Liberation
events.detail: On June 24, 1821, Simón Bolívar's patriot forces decisively defeated the Spanish Royalist army at the Battle of Carabobo, securing Venezuelan independence. General José Antonio Páez led a pivotal cavalry charge. Spanish forces retreated to Puerto Cabello and remained until 1823.

events.title: Gran Colombia Founded at Congress of Angostura
events.detail: Simón Bolívar proclaimed the Republic of Gran Colombia, uniting Venezuela, New Granada (Colombia), and later Ecuador. Bolívar served as president. The union was unstable from the start due to regional rivalries and military-caudillo tensions.

events.title: Venezuela Separates from Gran Colombia
events.detail: General José Antonio Páez led Venezuela's secession from Gran Colombia, establishing an independent republic. Bolívar died in December 1830, disillusioned by the fragmentation of his union. Páez became Venezuela's first de facto president (1830–1835).

events.title: Federal War Erupts
events.detail: Venezuela's deadliest civil conflict (1859–1863) pitted Liberal Federalists against Conservative Centralists. An estimated 200,000 died, devastated the agricultural economy, and ended with the Treaty of Coche (April 1863). The Liberals under Ezequiel Zamora and Juan Falcón eventually prevailed.

events.title: Guzmán Blanco Seizes Power — Modernization Era
events.detail: Antonio Guzmán Blanco (1870–1888) dominated Venezuelan politics for nearly two decades, imposing Liberal Autocracy. He built the capitol, telegraph lines, rail networks, and introduced compulsory primary education. Financed by Caracas and European creditors, his era laid infrastructure foundations but entrenched caudillo rule.

events.title: European Naval Blockade of Venezuela
events.detail: Britain, Germany, and Italy blockaded Venezuelan ports to collect unpaid debts from President Cipriano Castro. The U.S. invoked the Monroe Doctrine. The crisis led to the Drago Doctrine (1902) opposing European intervention for debt collection in the Americas and was a landmark in international law.

events.title: Juan Vicente Gómez Seizes Power
events.detail: General Juan Vicente Gómez overthrew President Cipriano Castro in a bloodless coup. Gómez ruled Venezuela for 27 years (1908–1935), transforming the country through oil revenues while maintaining a brutal police state. He paid off Venezuela's foreign debt entirely using oil income.

events.title: Oil Discovery at Maracaibo — Barroso II Well Blowout
events.detail: On December 14, 1922, the Barroso II well in the Maracaibo Basin erupted in a massive blowout, gushing 100,000 barrels per day and signaling Venezuela's transformation into a major oil producer. Royal Dutch Shell and Standard Oil rushed to secure concessions. Venezuela became the world's largest oil exporter by 1929.

events.title: Military Coup Ousts Rómulo Gallegos
events.detail: On November 24, 1948, a military junta overthrew President Rómulo Gallegos (AD) just nine months into his elected term. Marcos Pérez Jiménez emerged as supreme leader by 1952. His modernizing dictatorship (1948–1958) used oil revenues to fund infrastructure but brutally suppressed opposition through the secret police (Seguridad Nacional).

events.title: January 23 Revolution — Pérez Jiménez Ousted
events.detail: A popular uprising combined with a military coup forced Marcos Pérez Jiménez to flee Venezuela on January 23, 1958. A Patriotic Junta took power and pledged democratic elections. The date, January 23, remains a significant anniversary in Venezuelan political culture and is invoked by both government and opposition.

events.title: Punto Fijo Pact — Birth of Two-Party Democracy
events.detail: On October 31, 1958, Acción Democrática (AD), COPEI, and URD signed the Punto Fijo Pact, agreeing to respect election results and share power regardless of who won. The pact stabilized Venezuelan democracy for four decades but became associated with corruption and exclusion. Rómulo Betancourt won the December 1958 election.

events.title: PDVSA Nationalization of Oil Industry
events.detail: On January 1, 1976, under President Carlos Andrés Pérez (first term), Venezuela nationalized its oil industry. The state oil company PDVSA (Petróleos de Venezuela, S.A.) was created to manage operations previously controlled by ExxonMobil, Shell, Gulf, and other multinationals. At peak, PDVSA would become Venezuela's largest company and government revenue source.

events.title: El Caracazo — Riots and State Massacre
events.detail: On February 27–28, 1989, massive popular riots erupted in Caracas and major cities against President Carlos Andrés Pérez's IMF-mandated austerity measures (el Gran Viraje). Security forces killed an estimated 300–3,000 people. The events shattered the legitimacy of Punto Fijo democracy and radicalized a generation of military officers including Hugo Chávez.

events.title: Chávez Coup Attempt — February 4, 1992
events.detail: Lieutenant Colonel Hugo Chávez led a failed military coup (Movimiento Bolivariano Revolucionario 200) against President Carlos Andrés Pérez on February 4, 1992. The coup failed in Caracas though briefly succeeded in Valencia and Maracay. Chávez surrendered and gave a famous TV address taking sole responsibility ('por ahora'). He was imprisoned at Yare.

events.title: Hugo Chávez Elected President
events.detail: On December 6, 1998, Hugo Chávez won Venezuela's presidential election with 56.2% of the vote — the largest margin in four decades. Running on an anti-corruption, anti-Punto Fijo platform with promises of a new constitution, he defeated Henrique Salas Römer. His election marked the end of the AD-COPEI two-party system.

events.title: New Bolivarian Constitution Adopted
events.detail: Chávez called a constituent assembly that drafted a new constitution, approved in referendum (December 15, 1999) with 72% support. The constitution renamed the country the Bolivarian Republic of Venezuela, expanded social rights, extended presidential terms to 6 years, and created new state powers. It remains the legal framework invoked by both sides in all subsequent disputes.

events.title: Coup Attempt Against Chávez — April 11–13, 2002
events.detail: A coalition of military officers, business leaders (Fedecámaras), and the trade union CTV staged a coup on April 11, 2002, after large anti-Chávez marches were fired upon, killing 19. Pedro Carmona was briefly installed as president before pro-Chávez military and popular mobilizations reversed the coup on April 13. The US was later shown to have foreknowledge.

events.title: PDVSA Oil Strike — Economic Sabotage or Worker Revolt?
events.detail: From December 2002 to February 2003, PDVSA managers and opposition groups organized a two-month oil strike and lockout, cutting production from 3M bpd to near zero. The strike cost Venezuela $10 billion. Chávez fired 18,000 PDVSA employees and replaced management with political loyalists — a decision that accelerated the eventual technical collapse of PDVSA.

events.title: Chávez Survives Recall Referendum
events.detail: On August 15, 2004, a presidential recall referendum was held after the opposition collected sufficient signatures. Chávez won with 59% against recall. International observers (OAS, Carter Center) certified the result, though the opposition and some analysts disputed the CNE's handling. Chávez launched the Misión Barrio Adentro and other social programs with oil windfall.

events.title: Chávez Constitutional Reform Defeated by Referendum
events.detail: On December 2, 2007, Venezuelan voters rejected a package of 69 constitutional amendments proposed by Chávez, including indefinite re-election and expanded presidential powers, by a narrow 51%-49% margin. It was Chávez's first electoral defeat. He accepted the result, saying 'por ahora' — echoing his 1992 surrender speech. He subsequently pushed through term-limit removal via referendum in 2009.

events.title: Hugo Chávez Dies of Cancer
events.detail: On March 5, 2013, Hugo Chávez died in Caracas after a two-year battle with cancer (pelvic sarcoma). He had been re-elected in October 2012 with 55% of the vote — his final election. Vice-President Nicolás Maduro became acting president. Chávez's death triggered a constitutional succession crisis, a special election, and ultimately the unraveling of the Bolivarian economic model.

events.title: Maduro Wins Disputed Election by 1.5%
events.detail: On April 14, 2013, Nicolás Maduro defeated Henrique Capriles by a margin of 1.5% (50.6% vs. 49.1%) in the snap presidential election after Chávez's death. Capriles demanded a full recount, alleging fraud. The CNE conducted a limited audit. The result was recognized internationally but disputed domestically, establishing the pattern of contested elections that would define Venezuelan politics.

events.title: Guarimba Protests — 43 Killed, Leopoldo López Arrested
events.detail: In February 2014, opposition-led street protests ('guarimbas') erupted across Venezuela following deteriorating economic conditions. Over three months, 43 people were killed — by security forces, colectivos, and some protestors. Opposition leader Leopoldo López (Voluntad Popular) turned himself in to authorities on February 18 and was sentenced to nearly 14 years in prison in 2015 on charges widely condemned as politically motivated.

events.title: Opposition Wins Supermajority in National Assembly
events.detail: On December 6, 2015, the Democratic Unity Roundtable (MUD) coalition won a supermajority (112 of 167 seats) in the National Assembly — the first time in 16 years the opposition controlled the legislature. Maduro immediately moved to strip the assembly's powers, and the Supreme Court (TSJ) packed with chavistas ruled key legislative actions unconstitutional, triggering a constitutional standoff.

events.title: Maduro Creates Parallel Constituent Assembly
events.detail: After months of massive protests (April–July 2017, 125 killed) against his government, Maduro called a constituent assembly bypassing the opposition-controlled National Assembly. The July 30, 2017 vote was boycotted by the opposition and widely condemned. The Asamblea Nacional Constituyente (ANC) was seated but never ratified a new constitution, serving primarily as a parallel legislature.

events.title: Hyperinflation Peaks at 1,698,488%
events.detail: In 2018, Venezuela's inflation reached 1,698,488% according to the IMF — the worst hyperinflationary episode in the Western Hemisphere since the 1940s. The bolívar was redenominated in August 2018 (removing 5 zeros, creating the 'Bolívar Soberano'). Shortages of food, medicine, and basic goods became chronic. GDP shrank by more than 50% in five years.

events.title: Drone Attack on Maduro at Military Parade
events.detail: On August 4, 2018, two explosive-laden drones detonated near Nicolás Maduro during a military parade in Caracas. Maduro blamed the attack on Colombian President Juan Manuel Santos and the US. Several soldiers were injured. The group Soldados de Franelas claimed responsibility. The event was the first publicized drone assassination attempt on a sitting head of state.

events.title: Guaidó Declares Himself Interim President
events.detail: On January 23, 2019 — the anniversary of the 1958 revolution — National Assembly president Juan Guaidó declared himself interim president of Venezuela, citing Article 233 of the constitution. The US immediately recognized him, followed by 50+ countries. Maduro retained control of the military and state institutions. The resulting dual-presidency stalemate lasted until 2023.

events.title: Nationwide Blackout — Guri Dam Failure
events.detail: On March 7–14, 2019, Venezuela suffered its largest ever power outage, leaving nearly the entire country without electricity for up to 7 days. The blackout, caused by a cyberattack according to the government, or by infrastructure collapse due to neglect according to critics, disrupted water, hospitals, and communications. At least 26 people died in hospital during the outage. Subsequent smaller blackouts recurred throughout 2019.

events.title: Cúcuta Aid Bridge Standoff — Maduro Blocks Humanitarian Aid
events.detail: On February 23, 2019, Guaidó and Colombian President Duque staged a high-profile aid delivery attempt at the Tienditas Bridge on the Colombia-Venezuela border. Maduro ordered the military to block the convoy; soldiers burned trucks. USAID-shipped goods were turned back. Two people were killed in violence. The standoff highlighted the humanitarian crisis and the regime's resistance to international pressure.

events.title: US Sanctions PDVSA — Citgo Dispute Begins
events.detail: On January 28, 2019, the US Treasury Department (OFAC) imposed comprehensive sanctions on PDVSA, blocking US-based transactions and threatening third-country entities that did business with the Venezuelan state oil company. Citgo Petroleum (PDVSA's US subsidiary) was effectively transferred to Guaidó's government. The sanctions had severe knock-on effects on Venezuela's oil revenues and access to global financial markets.

events.title: Operation Libertad — Failed Military Uprising
events.detail: On April 30, 2019, Juan Guaidó surrounded himself with a small group of soldiers and opposition figures at La Carlota Air Force Base in Caracas, calling for a popular military uprising. The expected mass defection never materialized. Maduro retained military loyalty. Leopoldo López briefly left his Spanish Embassy refuge to join Guaidó, then sought asylum. The failure significantly weakened Guaidó's credibility.

events.title: Barbados Agreement Signed — Electoral Road Map
events.detail: On October 17, 2023, Venezuelan government and opposition negotiators signed the Barbados Agreement in Bridgetown, Barbados, brokered by Norway. The deal promised competitive presidential elections, release of some political prisoners, and asset unfreezing in exchange for sanctions relief. The US granted a 6-month oil license to Chevron. Maduro subsequently moved to bar opposition candidate María Corina Machado from running.

events.title: María Corina Machado Wins Opposition Primary
events.detail: On October 22, 2023, María Corina Machado won the Venezuelan opposition primary with over 90% of the vote, receiving 2.4 million ballots. She emerged as the dominant opposition leader. The Maduro government quickly moved to uphold an existing ban on her running for office, citing alleged corruption charges — which Machado and international observers denounced as politically motivated.

events.title: Disputed Presidential Election — Maduro Claims Victory
events.detail: Venezuela held presidential elections on July 28, 2024. The CNE declared Nicolás Maduro the winner within hours of polls closing, with official results never fully published. The opposition, led by candidate Edmundo González Urrutia (representing Machado's platform), reported that their witnesses collected 83% of actas (voting tallies) showing González winning with ~67% of the vote. The Carter Center, EU, OAS, and most Western governments refused to recognize the result.

events.title: Post-Election Crackdown — 25+ Killed, 2,400+ Arrested
events.detail: Following the disputed election result announcement on July 29–August 2024, nationwide protests erupted across Venezuela. Security forces and pro-government colectivos killed at least 25 demonstrators. The government arrested more than 2,400 people in the weeks following the election, including minors. Foro Penal documented hundreds of new political prisoners.

sources.name: UN High Commissioner for Human Rights

events.title: Edmundo González Flees to Spain
events.detail: On September 7, 2024, Edmundo González Urrutia fled Venezuela for Spain under diplomatic protection after Venezuelan authorities issued an arrest warrant. He was received in Madrid and granted asylum. The US, EU, and multiple Latin American governments recognized González as Venezuela's legitimately elected president. In October 2024, the US awarded him the Presidential Medal of Freedom.

events.title: Maduro Inaugurated for Third Term Amid Global Condemnation
events.detail: On January 10, 2025, Nicolás Maduro was inaugurated for a third six-year presidential term in Caracas. The ceremony was attended by allies including Nicaragua, Cuba, and Bolivia. The US, EU, and over 30 nations refused to recognize the inauguration. González Urrutia from Spain declared himself the legitimate constitutional president. Machado attempted to attend a parallel rally in Caracas but was briefly detained.
